whatToFeed: Acidifying organic fertilizer, such as cottonseed meal or a specialized azalea/rhododendron food, applied in early spring right as new growth begins.
pruningTips: Prune immediately after blooming concludes in late spring by snapping off spent flower trusses to encourage next year's buds; avoid pruning late in the season as it removes next spring's flowers.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Extremely toxic to humans, cats, dogs, and livestock.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant container-grown shrubs in early spring (late April) or early autumn (September) to allow root establishment in cool weather.
whenToHarvest: Cut full, vibrant flower clusters in late spring just as the lower buds on the cluster begin to unfurl for indoor vases.
usesForLargeHarvests: Stunning spring floral displays, long-lasting woodland landscape backdrops, privacy screening hedges.
hardinessNote: Extremely hardy perennial shrub; retains its thick, leathery leaves all winter, which curl inward to protect themselves during deep freezes.
plantingInstructions: Dig a shallow, wide hole twice the diameter of the root ball. Mix 50% acidic peat moss or aged pine bark into the native soil. Plant the shrub high, with the top of the root ball slightly above the surrounding soil line to ensure drainage, and cover with 3 inches of wood mulch.
growthRequirements: Demands moist, highly porous, well-aerated, and strictly acidic soil with a pH range between 4.5 and 5.5. Shallow, delicate root networks will rot instantly in stagnant standing water but dry out quickly in direct sun. Prefers dappled or morning sun with afternoon shade.

whatToFeed: Light application of a balanced, slow-release evergreen fertilizer in early spring; rarely requires heavy feeding.
pruningTips: Pruning is minimal and rarely necessary due to its naturally slow growth rate; light trimming to shape can be performed in early summer to remove rogue branches.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Grown strictly as a structural evergreen landscape asset.
combativePlants: Deep-shade plants that crowd its footprint
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant outdoors in early spring or early fall when temperatures are mild and rainfall is steady.
whenToHarvest: Not applicable; enjoyed strictly for its unique, year-round structural garden display.
usesForLargeHarvests: Excellent structural low hedge, rock garden focal point, or low-maintenance foundation framing accent.
hardinessNote: Incredibly cold-hardy evergreen perennial; handles harsh Western PA winter weather, snow loads, and winds with ease.
plantingInstructions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the root container but exactly the same depth. Place the shrub inside, ensuring the base sits flush with or slightly above the terrain line. Backfill with loose, non-amended native soil, pack firmly, and water deeply.
growthRequirements: Thrives best in average, cool, moist, but sharply draining soils with a preferred neutral to slightly acidic pH of 5.5 to 7.0. Quite drought-tolerant once established, though it cannot tolerate soggy, stagnant clay soils. Requires wide full sun.

whatToFeed: Light top-dress of organic compost or a balanced organic tree and shrub fertilizer in early spring.
pruningTips: Prune in late winter or early spring before new growth emerges to shape the bush, thin out interior branches for airflow, or maintain height boundaries.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Highly toxic to humans, dogs, cats, and horses.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant container starts in early spring or early fall to establish roots in cool, moist soil.
whenToHarvest: Clip striking, winged corky branches lined with brilliant red leaves in autumn for colorful floral arrangements.
usesForLargeHarvests: Stunning autumn crimson foliage focal points, dense privacy hedges, structural border boundaries.
hardinessNote: Deciduous perennial shrub; drops its leaves in winter after a spectacular fall color display.
plantingInstructions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the root mass. Unbound any circling roots gently. Plant at the same depth as the nursery container, fill with native soil, and firm down lightly. Water thoroughly and mulch the surrounding root footprint.
growthRequirements: Highly adaptable to a wide range of soil types, from sandy profiles to heavy regional clay, maintaining a pH of 6.0 to 7.5. Requires decent drainage. Complete full sun is mandatory to trigger the signature fiery crimson autumn foliage color change.

whatToFeed: Rarely needs heavy feeding; a light layer of compost around the base in spring is sufficient. Too much fertilizer promotes leaves over flowers.
pruningTips: Cut the entire shrub back hard to 12 inches above the ground in late winter or early spring; it blooms strictly on new wood. Deadhead spent flower spikes all summer to stimulate continuous blooming loops.
flavorProfile: Not edible. Primarily grown to anchor pollinator networks.
whenToPlant: Zone 6/7: Plant container-grown starts outdoors in spring (mid-to-late May) after frost danger clears.
whenToHarvest: Cut long, honey-scented cone-shaped flower spikes when they are 75% open on a dry morning for tall indoor vase displays.
usesForLargeHarvests: Fragrant cut floral arrangements, structural backdrop frames for pollinator gardens.
hardinessNote: Deciduous perennial shrub; drops foliage in winter and behaves almost like a herbaceous perennial by dying back significantly in cold northern climates.
plantingInstructions: Dig a hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide. Place the shrub level with the landscape surface. Backfill with a loose blend of native soil and organic compost, press down firmly to anchor the loose branches, and water deeply.
growthRequirements: Requires completely average, lean, loose, and exceptionally well-draining soil with a neutral pH of 6.5 to 7.5. Roots will rot instantly in damp, compact, or pooling water profiles. Demands absolute maximum full sun exposure to thrive.
